title: ML System Design
description: Recommendation, fraud, ranking, batch and real-time inference, feature stores, registries, and monitoring.

questions.title: Design a recommendation system.
questions.scenario: A platform team at a high-scale marketplace runs ML systems for ranking, fraud, recommendations, churn, and monitoring. A recent review found that a good model cannot be operated reliably at production scale; the system must support 50k daily users with p95 latency under 2.5 seconds, so the team needs a reliable approach for recommendation system measured by online lift, latency p95, model freshness, false-positive cost, and rollback time.
questions.expectedAnswer: recommendation system needs an end-to-end design that covers requirements, data flow, serving path, evaluation, and operations. In ML systems for ranking, fraud, recommendations, churn, and monitoring, the practical approach is requirements-driven architecture with clear data flow, failure handling, observability, security, and rollout. The first step is to establish a baseline with representative examples from events, labels, feature stores, model registry entries, online scores, and feedback loops, because without a baseline the team cannot distinguish real improvement from a better-looking demo. Next, implement the smallest production-shaped change using Kafka, Kubernetes, Redis, model registries, gateways, and OpenTelemetry, keeping enough trace data to inspect inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures. The main risk is demo-oriented design that ignores scale, permissions, cost, or operations; a strong design makes that risk visible through tests, monitoring, and review workflows. Validation should use online lift, latency p95, model freshness, false-positive cost, and rollback time, segmented by important user or data groups so averages do not hide regressions. The tradeoff is that stronger controls add complexity, latency, and ownership work, but they also reduce silent failure in production. A good interview answer explains the mechanism, the implementation path, the measurable success criteria, and the condition under which a simpler baseline is preferable.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: The scenario points to demo-oriented design that ignores scale, permissions, cost, or operations. recommendation system addresses that risk through requirements-driven architecture with clear data flow, failure handling, observability, security, and rollout, so the discussion should start with the observed production failure rather than a textbook definition. The first concrete step is to turn recent failures into a benchmark and compare the baseline against the proposed change using online lift, latency p95, model freshness, false-positive cost, and rollback time.
answerFramework.howToApply: Start with 50-200 representative examples from events, labels, feature stores, model registry entries, online scores, and feedback loops. Implement the change with Kafka, Kubernetes, Redis, model registries, gateways, and OpenTelemetry, keeping the old path available as a baseline. Log inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures so the team can debug regressions. Roll out behind a feature flag or pilot group, then expand only if quality and operational metrics improve.
answerFramework.example: Example: in ML systems for ranking, fraud, recommendations, churn, and monitoring, the baseline path accepts an input, produces a plausible output, but leaves no reliable evidence that the decision is correct. The improved path adds requirements-driven architecture with clear data flow, failure handling, observability, security, and rollout; it records the evidence, score, trace, or review status that supports the output. The final result is tied to validated signals instead of model confidence or notebook accuracy alone.
answerFramework.tradeoffs: The technique adds engineering overhead, evaluation work, and usually some latency or infrastructure cost. It is worthwhile when the failure affects trust, safety, revenue, or repeated manual effort. It is not worth overengineering for rare, low-risk tasks where a simple baseline already meets the target. Monitor online lift, latency p95, model freshness, false-positive cost, and rollback time so the team can tell whether the extra complexity pays for itself.

commonMistakes: Explaining recommendation system as a memorized definition without tying it to a production failure mode.
Skipping measurement and relying on anecdotal demo quality instead of online lift, latency p95, model freshness, false-positive cost, and rollback time.
Ignoring the main risk: demo-oriented design that ignores scale, permissions, cost, or operations.
Not discussing ownership, rollout, monitoring, and rollback when the approach fails in production.
followUpQuestions: What metric would prove that recommendation system improved the system rather than just changed it?
What failure mode would you expect after deploying recommendation system, and how would you detect it?
How would your answer change if latency, cost, or compliance became the primary constraint?
questions.realWorldExample: A platform team at a high-scale marketplace applied recommendation system in ML systems for ranking, fraud, recommendations, churn, and monitoring. The baseline suffered from demo-oriented design that ignores scale, permissions, cost, or operations, causing roughly 25% of reviewed cases to require manual correction. The team implemented requirements-driven architecture with clear data flow, failure handling, observability, security, and rollout using Kafka, Kubernetes, Redis, model registries, gateways, and OpenTelemetry, then evaluated the change against online lift, latency p95, model freshness, false-positive cost, and rollback time. After a limited rollout, manual corrections dropped by 12-18 percentage points and p95 latency stayed within the agreed operating budget.
questions.interviewerNotes: A strong candidate gives a concrete mechanism for recommendation system, names the data and metrics needed to validate it, and explains the operational risk. A great candidate also discusses rollout, observability, ownership, and when the added complexity is not justified.

questions.title: What problem does a feature store solve?
questions.scenario: A platform team at a high-scale marketplace runs ML systems for ranking, fraud, recommendations, churn, and monitoring. A recent review found that a good model cannot be operated reliably at production scale; the baseline fails on 20-35% of reviewed cases, so the team needs a reliable approach for What problem does a feature store solve measured by online lift, latency p95, model freshness, false-positive cost, and rollback time.
questions.expectedAnswer: What problem does a feature store solve belongs in the architecture discussion because it affects ownership boundaries, controls, observability, and rollout. In ML systems for ranking, fraud, recommendations, churn, and monitoring, the practical approach is production-safe feature design with leakage checks, freshness SLAs, and online/offline consistency. The first step is to establish a baseline with representative examples from events, labels, feature stores, model registry entries, online scores, and feedback loops, because without a baseline the team cannot distinguish real improvement from a better-looking demo. Next, implement the smallest production-shaped change using Feast, Spark, dbt, Great Expectations, and model monitoring, keeping enough trace data to inspect inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures. The main risk is features that work offline but are stale or unavailable in production; a strong design makes that risk visible through tests, monitoring, and review workflows. Validation should use online lift, latency p95, model freshness, false-positive cost, and rollback time, segmented by important user or data groups so averages do not hide regressions. The tradeoff is that stronger controls add complexity, latency, and ownership work, but they also reduce silent failure in production. A good interview answer explains the mechanism, the implementation path, the measurable success criteria, and the condition under which a simpler baseline is preferable.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: The scenario points to features that work offline but are stale or unavailable in production. What problem does a feature store solve addresses that risk through production-safe feature design with leakage checks, freshness SLAs, and online/offline consistency, so the discussion should start with the observed production failure rather than a textbook definition. The first concrete step is to turn recent failures into a benchmark and compare the baseline against the proposed change using online lift, latency p95, model freshness, false-positive cost, and rollback time.
answerFramework.howToApply: Start with 50-200 representative examples from events, labels, feature stores, model registry entries, online scores, and feedback loops. Implement the change with Feast, Spark, dbt, Great Expectations, and model monitoring, keeping the old path available as a baseline. Log inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures so the team can debug regressions. Roll out behind a feature flag or pilot group, then expand only if quality and operational metrics improve.
answerFramework.example: Example: in ML systems for ranking, fraud, recommendations, churn, and monitoring, the baseline path accepts an input, produces a plausible output, but leaves no reliable evidence that the decision is correct. Skipping measurement and relying on anecdotal demo quality instead of online lift, latency p95, model freshness, false-positive cost, and rollback time.
Ignoring the main risk: demo-oriented design that ignores scale, permissions, cost, or operations.
Not discussing ownership, rollout, monitoring, and rollback when the approach fails in production.
followUpQuestions: What metric would prove that feedback label pipeline improved the system rather than just changed it?
What failure mode would you expect after deploying feedback label pipeline, and how would you detect it?
How would your answer change if latency, cost, or compliance became the primary constraint?
questions.realWorldExample: A platform team at a high-scale marketplace applied feedback label pipeline in ML systems for ranking, fraud, recommendations, churn, and monitoring. The baseline suffered from demo-oriented design that ignores scale, permissions, cost, or operations, causing roughly 25% of reviewed cases to require manual correction. The team implemented requirements-driven architecture with clear data flow, failure handling, observability, security, and rollout using Kafka, Kubernetes, Redis, model registries, gateways, and OpenTelemetry, then evaluated the change against online lift, latency p95, model freshness, false-positive cost, and rollback time. After a limited rollout, manual corrections dropped by 12-18 percentage points and p95 latency stayed within the agreed operating budget.
questions.interviewerNotes: A strong candidate gives a concrete mechanism for feedback label pipeline, names the data and metrics needed to validate it, and explains the operational risk. A great candidate also discusses rollout, observability, ownership, and when the added complexity is not justified.

questions.title: Design around training-serving skew.
questions.scenario: A platform team at a high-scale marketplace runs ML systems for ranking, fraud, recommendations, churn, and monitoring. A recent review found that a good model cannot be operated reliably at production scale; the system must support 50k daily users with p95 latency under 2.5 seconds, so the team needs a reliable approach for training-serving skew measured by online lift, latency p95, model freshness, false-positive cost, and rollback time.
questions.expectedAnswer: training-serving skew needs an end-to-end design that covers requirements, data flow, serving path, evaluation, and operations. In ML systems for ranking, fraud, recommendations, churn, and monitoring, the practical approach is requirements-driven architecture with clear data flow, failure handling, observability, security, and rollout. The first step is to establish a baseline with representative examples from events, labels, feature stores, model registry entries, online scores, and feedback loops, because without a baseline the team cannot distinguish real improvement from a better-looking demo. Next, implement the smallest production-shaped change using Kafka, Kubernetes, Redis, model registries, gateways, and OpenTelemetry, keeping enough trace data to inspect inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures. The main risk is demo-oriented design that ignores scale, permissions, cost, or operations; a strong design makes that risk visible through tests, monitoring, and review workflows. Validation should use online lift, latency p95, model freshness, false-positive cost, and rollback time, segmented by important user or data groups so averages do not hide regressions. The tradeoff is that stronger controls add complexity, latency, and ownership work, but they also reduce silent failure in production. A good interview answer explains the mechanism, the implementation path, the measurable success criteria, and the condition under which a simpler baseline is preferable.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: The scenario points to demo-oriented design that ignores scale, permissions, cost, or operations. training-serving skew addresses that risk through requirements-driven architecture with clear data flow, failure handling, observability, security, and rollout, so the discussion should start with the observed production failure rather than a textbook definition. The first concrete step is to turn recent failures into a benchmark and compare the baseline against the proposed change using online lift, latency p95, model freshness, false-positive cost, and rollback time.
answerFramework.howToApply: Start with 50-200 representative examples from events, labels, feature stores, model registry entries, online scores, and feedback loops. Implement the change with Kafka, Kubernetes, Redis, model registries, gateways, and OpenTelemetry, keeping the old path available as a baseline. Log inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures so the team can debug regressions. Roll out behind a feature flag or pilot group, then expand only if quality and operational metrics improve.
answerFramework.example: Example: in ML systems for ranking, fraud, recommendations, churn, and monitoring, the baseline path accepts an input, produces a plausible output, but leaves no reliable evidence that the decision is correct. The improved path adds requirements-driven architecture with clear data flow, failure handling, observability, security, and rollout; it records the evidence, score, trace, or review status that supports the output. The final result is tied to validated signals instead of model confidence or notebook accuracy alone.
answerFramework.tradeoffs: The technique adds engineering overhead, evaluation work, and usually some latency or infrastructure cost. It is worthwhile when the failure affects trust, safety, revenue, or repeated manual effort. It is not worth overengineering for rare, low-risk tasks where a simple baseline already meets the target. Monitor online lift, latency p95, model freshness, false-positive cost, and rollback time so the team can tell whether the extra complexity pays for itself.

questions.title: Design around model rollback strategy.
questions.scenario: A platform team at a high-scale marketplace runs ML systems for ranking, fraud, recommendations, churn, and monitoring. A recent review found that a good model cannot be operated reliably at production scale; the system must support 50k daily users with p95 latency under 2.5 seconds, so the team needs a reliable approach for model rollback strategy measured by online lift, latency p95, model freshness, false-positive cost, and rollback time.
questions.expectedAnswer: model rollback strategy needs an end-to-end design that covers requirements, data flow, serving path, evaluation, and operations. In ML systems for ranking, fraud, recommendations, churn, and monitoring, the practical approach is model behavior control through decoding, grounding, evaluation, routing, and observability. The first step is to establish a baseline with representative examples from events, labels, feature stores, model registry entries, online scores, and feedback loops, because without a baseline the team cannot distinguish real improvement from a better-looking demo. Next, implement the smallest production-shaped change using OpenAI/Azure OpenAI, vLLM, LiteLLM, model gateways, and eval datasets, keeping enough trace data to inspect inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures. The main risk is fluent output that hides uncertainty, cost, or latency problems; a strong design makes that risk visible through tests, monitoring, and review workflows. Validation should use online lift, latency p95, model freshness, false-positive cost, and rollback time, segmented by important user or data groups so averages do not hide regressions. The tradeoff is that stronger controls add complexity, latency, and ownership work, but they also reduce silent failure in production. A good interview answer explains the mechanism, the implementation path, the measurable success criteria, and the condition under which a simpler baseline is preferable.

The first concrete step is to turn recent failures into a benchmark and compare the baseline against the proposed change using online lift, latency p95, model freshness, false-positive cost, and rollback time.
answerFramework.howToApply: Start with 50-200 representative examples from events, labels, feature stores, model registry entries, online scores, and feedback loops. Implement the change with OpenAI/Azure OpenAI, vLLM, LiteLLM, model gateways, and eval datasets, keeping the old path available as a baseline. Log inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures so the team can debug regressions. Roll out behind a feature flag or pilot group, then expand only if quality and operational metrics improve.
answerFramework.example: Example: in ML systems for ranking, fraud, recommendations, churn, and monitoring, the baseline path accepts an input, produces a plausible output, but leaves no reliable evidence that the decision is correct. The improved path adds model behavior control through decoding, grounding, evaluation, routing, and observability; it records the evidence, score, trace, or review status that supports the output. The final result is tied to validated signals instead of model confidence or notebook accuracy alone.
answerFramework.tradeoffs: The technique adds engineering overhead, evaluation work, and usually some latency or infrastructure cost. It is worthwhile when the failure affects trust, safety, revenue, or repeated manual effort. It is not worth overengineering for rare, low-risk tasks where a simple baseline already meets the target. Monitor online lift, latency p95, model freshness, false-positive cost, and rollback time so the team can tell whether the extra complexity pays for itself.
